content: I'm back from my sabbatical and ready to dive into some new interior design projects. I traveled through so much of Eastern Europe, it was incredible.
Welcome back! Eastern Europe has such a rich history and architecture. Did you find any inspiration for your design work during your travels?
Absolutely. The buildings in Prague and Budapest were stunning. I've been thinking of incorporating some of those historic elements into my next project, a commercial space in downtown Chicago.
That sounds like an exciting project. What kind of commercial space is it? And how do you plan to balance the historic elements with modern functionality?
It's going to be a restaurant. The client wants something unique that reflects the city's vibrant culture. I've been experimenting with woodworking to create custom pieces for the interior.
A restaurant project sounds like a lot of fun. Woodworking can add a nice personal touch. Are you considering any specific types of wood or designs that would complement the Chicago vibe?
I'm thinking of using reclaimed wood for the tables and bars. It adds a bit of history and warmth to the space. And, of course, I'll be brewing some of my own beer to test out the menu.
I've also been getting back into calligraphy, which I think could be a great way to add some decorative elements to the restaurant's signage and menus.

Reclaimed wood and custom calligraphy will definitely give the restaurant a unique character. How do you plan to manage your time between designing the space, brewing beer, and possibly even blogging about your travel experiences?
It's all about prioritizing and making time for the things I love. I've been cooking a lot of Thai food lately, too, which has been a great way to relax and get inspired for the restaurant project.

Thai food is delicious. Maybe you could even incorporate some of those dishes into the restaurant's menu. Have you set a specific opening date for the restaurant or is it still in the planning stages?
We're aiming for a summer opening, hopefully by July. It's going to be a lot of work, but I'm excited to see it all come together.
